my_thoughts: The differentiation works: harmless (daydreams) vs. dangerous (imagining qualities/false values in self/others), general remedy = self-remembering. The student is intellectualizing obstacle (identifies obstacle), which shows they're seeking special method. They'll likely ask analytic question next. Hypothesis: intellectualizing obstacles trigger analytic questions after differentiation—testable by tracking whether obstacle identifications lead to analytic inquiries.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student identifies obstacle ('I feel my chief obstacle is imagination, chiefly about myself. Is there any special work against this?')—intellectualizing obstacle via identifies obstacle and asks for special method. They're seeking special method for imagination. The comparison_contrast mechanism differentiates: harmless (daydreams) vs. dangerous (imagining qualities/false values in self/others), general remedy = self-remembering. Pattern: obstacle identification → differentiation → [next: analytic question]. Medium confidence because differentiations typically lead to compliance, but intellectualizing obstacles may trigger analytic questions.
visible_response:
Imagination may be very different. If there are special ways, it is only
possible to arrive at them through a detailed study.
If you ask me in
general, I can only answer—self-remembering.
If you mean day-dreams, they are only half of our imaginings, and the most innocent
half.
Certainly they mean loss of time, but we waste time in so many ways that a little
more or less does not make much difference.
It is much more dangerous if you imagine
certain qualities in yourself in other people, in humanity or in nature, and then come to
believe in these imaginary ideas and put your faith in them.
We are surrounded by these
imaginary qualities, and it is with these that we must struggle.
